title: Chapter 32: The Reformation Begins
content: The audit of the First Seat's voting record began the next day.

Frost handled the logistics. The Frost-bringer had been waiting for this opportunity since the moment it touched the black card. It spent the morning in the Exchange's central archive, cross-referencing forty thousand years of transactions against the First Seat's voting history.

"The First Seat has voted on 847 measures in the past millennium," Frost reported. "Of those, 612 directly benefited the Exchange. 203 indirectly benefited the Exchange. 32 were neutral. Zero were opposed to Exchange interests."
Roxanne looked at the numbers. "So the First Seat has been using the Council to rubber-stamp Exchange policies for a thousand years."
"Correct."
"And no one noticed?"
"The First Seat is the First Seat. No one audits the auditor."
"Until now."
"Until now."
She set the report down. "Release it. All of it."
"To whom?"
"To everyone. All Council members. All Exchange territories. The Deep Paths. The Crossroads. Every entity that's ever wondered why the Exchange always wins."
Frost's hood tilted. "That is a declaration of war."
"War was declared the moment I bought the campus. This is just the first battle."
The report spread through the Nether-Grid like wildfire.

Not literal wildfire — the Nether-Grid didn't have fire in the traditional sense. But information traveled faster than any flame. Within hours, every Collector, every trader, every debt-holder in the Exchange knew: the First Seat had been compromised. The audit was public.
In the Upper Chamber, the First Seat's presence had withdrawn into silence. It did not call a session. It did not issue statements. It simply... waited.
"The First Seat is calculating," the Seventh Seat observed. It had appeared in Roxanne's office via a projection of light. "It will not respond immediately. It will wait for you to make a mistake."
"What kind of mistake?"

"Overreach. The report damages its reputation, but reputation is not power. The First Seat still controls the Exchange's enforcement division. It still has allies in the Council. One audit does not unseat a Primordial."
"I'm not trying to unseat it. I'm trying to reform the system."
"Those are the same thing to the First Seat."
Roxanne considered this.
"Then let it think I'm trying to unseat it. While I'm actually building something it can't touch."
The next day, Roxanne announced the Vance Demesne's first public works project.
A bridge.

Not a physical bridge. A commercial bridge — a trade route that connected the Deep Paths directly to the surface Nether-Grid, bypassing the Exchange entirely. Any entity could use it. No tolls. No debt. No contracts.
"Free trade?" Lena asked, sketchbook in hand. "In the apocalypse?"
"The apocalypse is over. The Nether-Grid is just a system now. And systems can be changed."
"By building a bridge?"
"By building an alternative. The Exchange controls the roads. So I'm building a new road."
Lena looked at the plans Frost had drawn — schematics of dimensional coordinates and trade flow projections.
"You're really doing this."
"I'm really doing this."
"Can I draw the opening ceremony?"
Roxanne smiled. "You can draw the whole thing."
The bridge opened three days later.

It wasn't a grand structure. It was a stabilized dimensional corridor — a permanent portal between the Crossroads in the Deep Paths and the main quad of Blackwood University. Frost had calculated the coordinates. Silas had stabilized the aperture with a fragment of his restored power.
Entity traffic began immediately.

Traders from the Deep Paths stepped through with goods that had never been seen on the surface — crystallized starlight, bottled silence, fabrics woven from shadows that moved when you weren't looking. Refugees traded Infinity Notes for supplies they hadn't had access to in months.
Roxanne stood at the edge of the portal, watching.
"The Exchange will try to destroy it," Silas said.
"Let them try."
"You keep saying that."
"Because it keeps being true."
She turned to look at the flow of traffic — beings from a hundred dimensions, exchanging goods without debt, without fear, without the Exchange taking its cut.
"This is what the Void looked like," she said quietly. "Before it collapsed."
Silas was silent for a moment.
"How do you know?"
"I don't. But I can feel it. The card remembers."
